title: Where do you get free historical tick-level trades and full L2 order book data?
author: DayNo4131
selftext:
I’m looking for historical market data at tick resolution. By that I mean every individual trade and full L2 order book data, either snapshots or updates.

For any serious work, manually collecting tick data usually means running a service continuously, storing everything in a database, and then waiting months before you even have enough history to work with. That approach makes sense in production, but it’s a big barrier when you just want historical data upfront for research or backtesting.

So far, the only exchange I’ve found that offers this kind of historical data for free is Bybit:  
[https://www.bybit.com/derivatives/en/history-data](https://www.bybit.com/derivatives/en/history-data)

Credit where it’s due. The data quality looks high, coverage is good, and access to both trades and deep L2 order books without enterprise pricing is rare.

The downside is that downloading it manually is extremely annoying. Because of that, I built a small automated fetcher that simply automates the annoying UI clicks such as symbol selection, date ranges, and chunking. It doesn’t unlock new access, it just makes the existing process tolerable.  
[https://github.com/flowdrivenml/bybit-history-downloader/](https://github.com/flowdrivenml/bybit-history-downloader/)

What I really want to know is this:

Are there any other sources people use for free or realistically affordable historical tick-level trade data or full L2 order book data?

I’m especially curious about exchanges where microstructure inefficiencies tend to be larger, and where historical order book or tick-level data is accessible without enterprise contracts or NDAs.

Any pointers, even partial datasets or sources with caveats, would be hugely appreciated.

Thanks

comments.author: TMJ848
comments.body: I use Birt’s Tick Data Suite

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body: Thank you for answering. Do you have some links?

comments.author: WealthMan11
comments.body: Free? Forget it. If you're going to backtest and/or use tick data, then you need a solid paid provider for that.  I use IQFeed (about $109/month for the basic subscription with 500 concurrent streams). WealthLab has a free IQFeed adapter for it on the Extensions page and updates can be automated.  When you download ticks, they're cached on the machine so as not to require another download for the same data, which as you know can be time consuming for very liquid instruments. L2 is available, but not historically, so it's not part of the backtesting equation. What's your plan for L2?  Are you doing directed trading?  Which broker?  Which instruments?

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body: I do not like your answer. Not because you are trying to sell me iqfeed substiption, but because you mentioned "backtesting".

replies.author: UnionCounty22
replies.body: Then what would you call running trading tests on historical data oh wise one

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body: Think of it like ML: training and tuning come first, backtesting is the test set — no more parameter changes.

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body:
Brute force is a fast way to generate false discoveries.

Most of my baselines are tree-based (random forest, XGBoost) using features derived from trade ticks and order-book snapshots: flow, depth changes, imbalance, cancellations vs reinforcements, vpins. 

Good luck in your signal generation platform.

replies.author: UnionCounty22
replies.body: Thanks & those are pretty cool too. I saw one called a GARCH architecture. Apparently it’s a good regime and volatility model

replies.author: DayNo4131
replies.body:
Well, yes and no. Garch is kinda like a stickier version of simple realized volatility with more hyperparameters to optimize, which is another pain in the ass. 

For what I do, I would say simple EWMA volatility gives 90% of the benefits with 5% of care compared to GARCH.

For imbalance / event-based bars, volatility is already normalized by the sampling, so EWMA (or nothing) is usually sufficient. GARCH doesn’t add much.

For fixed time-frame bars, volatility clustering is not absorbed by sampling, so GARCH can meaningfully improve volatility estimates, especially for sizing, labeling, or regime conditioning.

comments.author: Necessary_Cry_2546
comments.body: I provide custom historical depth datasets for quantitative research”
